sentences.zh: 太史公曰：自古聖王將建國受命，興動事業，何嘗不寶卜筮以助善！

translations.literal: The Grand Historian says: Since ancient times, when sage kings were about to establish states and receive the Mandate, and initiate great enterprises, how could they not treasure divination by tortoise shell and milfoil to aid what is good!
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian remarks: Since antiquity, whenever sage kings set about founding states, receiving the Mandate of Heaven, and launching great enterprises, they invariably prized the arts of divination by tortoise shell and milfoil stalks to guide them toward the good.

translations.literal: Before the times of Tang and Yu, records cannot be made.
translations.idiomatic: Of the ages before Yao and Shun, no records survive.

translations.literal: Since the rise of the Three Dynasties, each relied on auspicious omens.
translations.idiomatic: From the rise of the Three Dynasties onward, each founded its legitimacy on auspicious omens.

sentences.zh: 涂山之兆從而夏啟世，飛燕之卜順故殷興，百穀之筮吉故周王。

translations.literal: The omen at Tushan was favorable so the Xia began its rule; the divination of the flying swallow was auspicious so the Yin arose; the milfoil reading of the hundred grains was fortunate so the Zhou became kings.
translations.idiomatic: The omen at Mount Tu proved favorable, and so the Xia established its line of succession; the augury of the flying swallow was auspicious, and so the Yin dynasty arose; the milfoil divination of the hundred grains returned a fortunate result, and so the house of Zhou ascended to kingship.

sentences.zh: 王者決定諸疑，參以卜筮，斷以蓍龜，不易之道也。

translations.literal: When kings decide doubtful matters, they consult divination by tortoise shell and milfoil, and make judgments by milfoil stalks and tortoise shells — this is an unchanging principle.
translations.idiomatic: For kings to resolve matters of doubt by consulting the milfoil and the tortoise shell, and to render judgment through these instruments — this is an immutable principle.

sentences.zh: 蠻夷氐羌雖無君臣之序，亦有決疑之卜。

translations.literal: The Man, Yi, Di, and Qiang barbarians, though they lack the proper order of ruler and minister, also have divination for resolving doubts.
translations.idiomatic: Even the Man, Yi, Di, and Qiang peoples, though they lack the hierarchical order of ruler and minister, nonetheless possess their own methods of divination for resolving uncertainties.

sentences.zh: 或以金石，或以草木，國不同俗。

translations.literal: Some use metal and stone, some use plants and wood — different states have different customs.
translations.idiomatic: Some divine with metal and stone, others with grasses and wood — the customs differ from one people to the next.

sentences.zh: 然皆可以戰伐攻擊，推兵求勝，各信其神，以知來事。

translations.literal: Yet all can be used for warfare and attack, deploying troops to seek victory; each trusts its spirits to know future events.
translations.idiomatic: Yet all these methods serve the purposes of warfare and military campaigns, of deploying armies and seeking victory; each people trusts in its own spirits to foretell what is to come.

sentences.zh: 略聞夏殷欲卜者，乃取蓍龜，已則棄去之，以為龜藏則不靈，蓍久則不神。

translations.literal: It is roughly heard that in Xia and Yin, those who wished to divine would take milfoil and tortoise shells, and after use would discard them, believing that if tortoise shells were stored they would lose their spiritual power, and if milfoil stalks were kept too long they would lose their divinity.
translations.idiomatic: We hear that in the Xia and Yin periods, those who wished to perform divination would procure tortoise shells and milfoil stalks, then discard them after use, believing that a tortoise shell lost its numinous power if stored away, and milfoil stalks their spiritual efficacy if kept too long.

sentences.zh: 至周室之卜官，常寶藏蓍龜；

translations.literal: By the time of the Zhou court's divination officials, milfoil stalks and tortoise shells were always treasured and stored;
translations.idiomatic: By the time of the Zhou dynasty, however, the court's divination officials began to treasure and preserve the milfoil stalks and tortoise shells;

sentences.zh: 又其大小先後，各有所尚，要其歸等耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, regarding their size, order, and precedence, each had its own preferences, but their ultimate purpose was the same.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, though there were particular preferences regarding the size, order, and priority of the instruments, in the end their purpose was one and the same.

sentences.zh: 或以為聖王遭事無不定，決疑無不見，其設稽神求問之道者，以為後世衰微，愚不師智，人各自安，化分為百室，道散而無垠，故推歸之至微，要絜於精神也。

translations.literal: Some believe that sage kings encountered no affair they could not settle and resolved no doubt they could not see through; that they established the method of consulting the spirits and seeking answers because in later ages the world would decline, the foolish would not learn from the wise, each person would be content with himself, civilization would fracture into a hundred households, and the Way would scatter without limit; therefore they traced matters back to the most subtle and bound them to the realm of the spirit.
translations.idiomatic: Some hold that sage kings were able to settle every affair and see through every uncertainty, and that they established the practice of consulting spirits and seeking answers precisely because they foresaw a future age of decline — when the foolish would no longer learn from the wise, when each person would rest content in his own ways, when civilization would splinter into a hundred separate households, and the Way would scatter beyond all bounds. And so they traced the source of guidance back to the most subtle essence, binding it to the realm of the spirit.

sentences.zh: 或以為昆蟲之所長，聖人不能與爭。

translations.literal: Some believe that what insects and creatures excel at, even sages cannot compete with.
translations.idiomatic: Others maintain that in the special faculties of insects and creatures, even sages cannot rival them.

sentences.zh: 其處吉凶，別然否，多中於人。

translations.literal: Their determinations of fortune and misfortune, distinguishing right from wrong, often prove more accurate than those of humans.
translations.idiomatic: Their ability to determine fortune and misfortune, to distinguish what will come to pass from what will not, often proves more accurate than human judgment.

translations.literal: By the time of Emperor Gaozu, the Qin Grand Diviner's office was continued.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Gaozu founded the Han, he retained the office of Grand Diviner from the Qin dynasty.

translations.literal: The realm had just been settled, and arms and warfare had not yet ceased.
translations.idiomatic: The empire had only just been pacified, and the clamor of war had not yet subsided.

sentences.zh: 及孝惠享國日少，呂后女主，孝文、孝景因襲掌故，未遑講試，雖父子疇官，世世相傳，其精微深妙，多所遺失。

translations.literal: When Emperor Hui enjoyed the state for but few days, Empress Lü ruled as a female sovereign, and Emperors Wen and Jing followed established precedents without finding time to study and test the art — though fathers and sons held the office hereditarily, passing it down generation after generation, much of its subtlety and profundity was lost.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui's reign was brief, Empress Lü governed as regent, and Emperors Wen and Jing simply followed inherited precedents without finding the leisure to study or practice the divinatory arts. Though the office was passed from father to son through the generations, much of its subtle and profound knowledge was lost along the way.

sentences.zh: 至今上即位，博開藝能之路，悉延百端之學，通一伎之士咸得自效，絕倫超奇者為右，無所阿私，數年之閒，太卜大集。

translations.literal: When the present emperor ascended the throne, he broadly opened the paths of arts and skills, extended invitations to all branches of learning, allowed those proficient in any one art to demonstrate their abilities, placed the extraordinarily talented at the head, showing no favoritism — within a few years, the Grand Divination office was greatly assembled.
translations.idiomatic: When the present Emperor ascended the throne, he threw open every avenue for talent and skill, welcomed scholars of every discipline, and allowed anyone with mastery of even a single art to demonstrate his abilities. Those of surpassing and extraordinary talent were elevated to the highest positions, with no favoritism shown. Within just a few years, the office of Grand Divination was fully restored.

sentences.zh: 會上欲擊匈奴，西攘大宛，南收百越，卜筮至預見表象，先圖其利。

translations.literal: It happened that the emperor wished to strike the Xiongnu, drive back Dayuan in the west, and subjugate the Baiyue in the south; divination was used to foresee portents and plan advantages in advance.
translations.idiomatic: It so happened that the Emperor wished to strike the Xiongnu, push back Dayuan in the west, and bring the Baiyue peoples of the south under submission. Divination was employed to foresee omens and portents, allowing him to plan his advantages in advance.

sentences.zh: 及猛將推鋒執節，獲勝於彼，而蓍龜時日亦有力於此。

translations.literal: When fierce generals pressed their blades forward and held fast their standards, winning victories in those lands, the milfoil and tortoise shells and auspicious days also lent their power here.
translations.idiomatic: As fierce generals drove their blades forward and held their battle standards firm, winning victories in distant lands, the milfoil and tortoise shell divinations and the selection of auspicious days lent their own force to these triumphs.

sentences.zh: 上尤加意，賞賜至或數千萬。

translations.literal: The emperor especially paid attention, and rewards and gifts sometimes reached tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: The Emperor took a particular interest, and the rewards he bestowed sometimes amounted to tens of millions of cash.

sentences.zh: 如丘子明之屬，富溢貴寵，傾於朝廷。

translations.literal: People like Qiu Ziming and his kind overflowed with wealth and basked in honor and favor, dominating the court.
translations.idiomatic: Men such as Qiu Ziming and others of his kind amassed overflowing wealth and basked in such honor and imperial favor that they dominated the entire court.

sentences.zh: 至以卜筮射蠱道，巫蠱時或頗中。

translations.literal: It went so far as using divination to detect sorcery and the Way of gu-poison; the witchcraft divinations sometimes proved quite accurate.
translations.idiomatic: The practice even extended to using divination to ferret out sorcery and gu-poison; these witchcraft divinations at times proved remarkably accurate.

sentences.zh: 素有啊〃幀×因公行誅，恣意所傷，以破族滅門者，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Those who had prior grudges would use official punishments to carry out executions, injuring whomever they wished; those whose clans were destroyed and households extinguished were beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: Those who bore old grudges seized on these official proceedings to carry out their vengeance, harming whomever they pleased. The families destroyed and households exterminated in this manner were beyond counting.

sentences.zh: 百僚蕩恐，皆曰龜策能言。

translations.literal: The hundred officials were shaken with fear, all saying the tortoise shells and milfoil stalks could speak.
translations.idiomatic: Every official in the court trembled with fear, all declaring that the tortoise shells and milfoil stalks could truly speak.

translations.literal: Later, when the affairs were discovered and the treachery was fully exposed, the perpetrators were also punished to the third degree of kinship.
translations.idiomatic: When the truth eventually came to light and the full extent of the treachery was exposed, the perpetrators and three generations of their kin were put to death.

sentences.zh: 夫摓策定數，灼龜觀兆，變化無窮，是以擇賢而用占焉，可謂聖人重事者乎！

translations.literal: Now, manipulating milfoil stalks to determine numbers, scorching tortoise shells to observe cracks — the transformations are endless; therefore one selects the worthy to perform divination — may this be called how sages treat matters with gravity!
translations.idiomatic: To manipulate the milfoil stalks and fix the numbers, to scorch the tortoise shell and read the cracks — the permutations are inexhaustible. It is for this reason that only men of worth are chosen to perform divination. Truly, this shows how gravely the sages regarded such matters!

sentences.zh: 周公卜三龜，而武王有瘳。

translations.literal: The Duke of Zhou divined with three tortoise shells, and King Wu recovered from illness.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou performed divination with three tortoise shells, and King Wu recovered from his illness.

translations.literal: Zhou was cruel and tyrannical, and the great tortoise did not respond to divination.
translations.idiomatic: King Zhou of Shang was cruel and tyrannical, and the great tortoise refused to yield its oracle.

sentences.zh: 晉文將定襄王之位，卜得黃帝之兆，卒受彤弓之命。

translations.literal: When Duke Wen of Jin was about to secure the throne for King Xiang, he divined and obtained the omen of the Yellow Emperor, and ultimately received the mandate of the vermilion bow.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Wen of Jin set out to secure King Xiang's throne, his divination returned the omen of the Yellow Emperor, and in the end he received the mandate symbolized by the vermilion bow.

sentences.zh: 獻公貪驪姬之色，卜而兆有口象，其禍竟流五世。

translations.literal: Duke Xian of Jin coveted the beauty of Lady Li, and when he divined the omen showed the image of a mouth; the resulting calamity flowed on for five generations.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Xian of Jin, besotted with the beauty of Lady Li, consulted the oracle, which returned the omen of a mouth — a warning that the resulting calamity would plague his house for five generations.

sentences.zh: 楚靈將背周室，卜而龜逆，終被乾谿之敗。

translations.literal: King Ling of Chu was about to turn his back on the Zhou court, but when he divined the tortoise shell was unfavorable; he ultimately suffered defeat at Ganxi.
translations.idiomatic: When King Ling of Chu resolved to defy the Zhou court, his tortoise shell divination returned an adverse result. In the end, he met his ruin at Ganxi.

sentences.zh: 兆應信誠於內，而時人明察見之於外，可不謂兩合者哉！

translations.literal: The omens responded truthfully and faithfully within, and perceptive people of the time clearly observed them from without — can this not be called a perfect convergence of the two!
translations.idiomatic: The omens spoke truthfully from within, and the discerning observers of the age recognized their meaning from without — can one not call this a perfect convergence of the inner and the outer?

sentences.zh: 君子謂夫輕卜筮，無神明者，悖；

translations.literal: The gentleman says that those who take divination lightly and deny the spirits are perverse;
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman holds that those who make light of divination and deny the existence of spirits are perverse;

sentences.zh: 背人道，信禎祥者，鬼神不得其正。

translations.literal: those who abandon the Way of humanity and trust only in auspicious omens cause the ghosts and spirits to lose their proper place.
translations.idiomatic: yet those who abandon the Way of humanity and place blind faith in omens alone cause the spirits to lose their proper function.

sentences.zh: 故書建稽疑，五謀而卜筮居其二，五占從其多，明有而不專之道也。

translations.literal: Therefore the Documents established the method of consulting on doubts: of the five deliberations, divination by tortoise and milfoil constitute two; of the five prognostications one follows the majority — making clear the way of having recourse to divination without relying on it exclusively.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the Book of Documents established the method for resolving doubt: of the five deliberations, tortoise shell and milfoil divination account for two; among the five prognostications, one follows the majority. This makes clear the principle of employing divination without relying upon it exclusively.

sentences.zh: 余至江南，觀其行事，問其長老，云龜千歲乃遊蓮葉之上，蓍百莖共一根。

translations.literal: I went to the region south of the Yangtze, observed their practices, and questioned the elders there, who said that turtles a thousand years old swim upon lotus leaves, and that a hundred stalks of milfoil share a single root.
translations.idiomatic: I traveled to the lands south of the Yangtze, observed the local practices, and questioned the elders. They told me that turtles a thousand years old swim upon lotus leaves, and that a hundred milfoil stalks may spring from a single root.

sentences.zh: 又其所生，獸無虎狼，草無毒螫。

translations.literal: Moreover, in the places where they grow, among the animals there are no tigers or wolves, and among the plants there are no poisonous or stinging ones.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, in the places where these grow, there are no tigers or wolves among the animals, and no poisonous or stinging plants among the grasses.

sentences.zh: 江傍家人常畜龜飲食之，以為能導引致氣，有益於助衰養老，豈不信哉！

translations.literal: The households along the river often raise turtles and feed them, believing they can guide and attract vital energy, and are beneficial for aiding the frail and nourishing the aged — is this not credible!
translations.idiomatic: Households along the river commonly raise turtles and keep them fed, believing the creatures can channel vital energy and benefit the frail and elderly. How can one say this is not credible!

sentences.zh: 褚先生曰：臣以通經術，受業博士，治春秋，以高第為郎，幸得宿衛，出入宮殿中十有餘年。

translations.literal: Master Chu says: Your servant, through mastery of the classical arts, studied under the Erudites, specialized in the Spring and Autumn Annals, and by virtue of high examination results was appointed a Gentleman; I was fortunate to serve as a palace guard, entering and leaving the palace for over ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Master Chu says: I gained my appointment through mastery of the classical arts, studying under the court Erudites and specializing in the Spring and Autumn Annals. Thanks to a high ranking on the examinations, I was made a Gentleman of the palace and had the privilege of serving as a guard, coming and going within the imperial palace for more than ten years.

translations.literal: I privately admired the Grand Historian's records.
translations.idiomatic: I have always been a devoted admirer of the Grand Historian's records.

sentences.zh: 太史公之傳曰：「三王不同龜，四夷各異卜，然各以決吉凶，略闚其要，故作龜策列傳。」

translations.literal: The Grand Historian's account states: 'The three kings used different tortoise shells, and the four barbarian peoples each had their own divination methods; yet all used them to determine fortune and misfortune. Having briefly glimpsed their essentials, I therefore composed the Treatise on Tortoise and Milfoil Divination.'
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Historian's account states: 'The three ancient kings each employed different tortoise shells, and the four barbarian peoples each practiced their own methods of divination; yet all used these arts to determine fortune and misfortune. Having gained a glimpse of their essentials, I composed the Treatise on Tortoise and Milfoil Divination.'

sentences.zh: 臣往來長安中，求龜策列傳不能得，故之大卜官，問掌故文學長老習事者，寫取龜策卜事，編于下方。

translations.literal: Your servant went back and forth in Chang'an, seeking the Treatise on Tortoise and Milfoil Divination but could not obtain it; therefore I went to the Grand Diviner's office, questioned the custodians, literary scholars, and elderly practitioners experienced in these matters, copied the turtle shell and milfoil divination practices, and compiled them below.
translations.idiomatic: I went back and forth through Chang'an searching for the Treatise on Tortoise and Milfoil Divination, but could not find it. So I went to the office of the Grand Diviner and questioned the custodians of precedent, the scholars, and the elderly practitioners versed in these matters. From them I copied the practices of tortoise and milfoil divination and compiled them in what follows.

sentences.zh: 聞古五帝、三王發動舉事，必先決蓍龜。

translations.literal: It is heard that when the ancient Five Emperors and Three Kings initiated affairs and launched enterprises, they always first consulted the milfoil and tortoise.
translations.idiomatic: It is said that whenever the Five Emperors and Three Kings of antiquity set any enterprise in motion, they invariably consulted the milfoil and the tortoise shell first.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「下有伏靈，上有兔絲；

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'Below there lies a hidden spirit, and above there grows dodder;
translations.idiomatic: The tradition states: 'Where a hidden spirit dwells below, dodder grows above;

translations.literal: Above there is pounded milfoil, below there is a divine tortoise.'
translations.idiomatic: Above, there is milfoil being pounded; below, there dwells a divine tortoise.'

sentences.zh: 所謂伏靈者，在兔絲之下，狀似飛鳥之形。

translations.literal: The so-called 'hidden spirit' lies beneath the dodder, and its shape resembles that of a bird in flight.
translations.idiomatic: The so-called 'hidden spirit' is found beneath the dodder vine, and its shape resembles a bird in flight.

sentences.zh: 新雨已，天清靜無風，以夜捎兔絲去之，既以篝燭此地燭之，火滅，即記其處，以新布四丈環置之，明即掘取之，入四尺至七尺，得矣，過七尺不可得。

translations.literal: After a fresh rain, when the sky is clear and still with no wind, at night one strips away the dodder and removes it; then one lights a cage-lantern to illuminate the spot; when the flame goes out, one marks the place, surrounds it with four zhang of new cloth, and at dawn digs it up — at a depth of four to seven chi one obtains it; beyond seven chi it cannot be found.
translations.idiomatic: After a fresh rain, when the sky is clear, still, and windless, one goes out at night to strip away the dodder. A caged lantern is then set up to illuminate the spot. When the flame dies, one marks the place and encircles it with four zhang of new cloth. At dawn, one digs — at a depth of four to seven chi, one will find it. Below seven chi, it cannot be obtained.

sentences.zh: 伏靈者，千歲松根也，食之不死。

translations.literal: The 'hidden spirit' is the root of a pine tree a thousand years old; eating it confers immortality.
translations.idiomatic: The 'hidden spirit' is the root of a thousand-year-old pine. Whoever eats it will never die.

sentences.zh: 聞蓍生滿百莖者，其下必有神龜守之，其上常有青雲覆之。

translations.literal: It is heard that where milfoil grows to a full hundred stalks, below there must be a divine tortoise guarding it, and above there is always a blue-green cloud covering it.
translations.idiomatic: It is said that wherever milfoil grows to a full hundred stalks, a divine tortoise invariably guards it from below, and an azure cloud perpetually hovers above it.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「天下和平，王道得，而蓍莖長丈，其叢生滿百莖。」

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'When the realm is at peace and the kingly Way prevails, then milfoil stalks grow to a zhang in length and their clusters reach a full hundred stalks.'
translations.idiomatic: The tradition states: 'When the realm is at peace and the kingly Way prevails, the milfoil stalks grow to a full zhang in length, and their clusters number a hundred stalks.'

sentences.zh: 方今世取蓍者，不能中古法度，不能得滿百莖長丈者，取八十莖已上，蓍長八尺，即難得也。

translations.literal: In the present age, those who gather milfoil cannot match the ancient standards, and cannot obtain clusters of a full hundred stalks a zhang long; to gather eighty stalks or more, with milfoil eight chi long, is already difficult.
translations.idiomatic: In the present age, those who gather milfoil cannot meet the ancient standards. Finding a full cluster of a hundred stalks, a zhang in length, is impossible; even gathering eighty or more stalks, eight chi long, is already difficult.

sentences.zh: 人民好用卦者，取滿六十莖已上，長滿六尺者，既可用矣。

translations.literal: Common people who like to use hexagrams take stalks numbering sixty or more and six chi or more in length — these can already be used.
translations.idiomatic: Ordinary folk who wish to cast hexagrams may use clusters of sixty stalks or more, six chi or longer — these are sufficient for the purpose.

sentences.zh: 記曰：「能得名龜者，財物歸之，家必大富至千萬。」

translations.literal: The record says: 'One who can obtain a famous tortoise will have wealth and goods flow to him, and his household will surely grow enormously rich, reaching ten million.'
translations.idiomatic: The records state: 'Whoever obtains a famous tortoise will see wealth flow toward him, and his household is certain to amass a fortune reaching ten million.'

sentences.zh: 一曰「北斗龜」，二曰「南辰龜」，三曰「五星龜」，四曰「八風龜」，五曰「二十八宿龜」，六曰「日月龜」，七曰「九州龜」，八曰「玉龜」：凡八名龜。

translations.literal: The first is called the 'Northern Dipper Tortoise,' the second the 'Southern Star Tortoise,' the third the 'Five Planets Tortoise,' the fourth the 'Eight Winds Tortoise,' the fifth the 'Twenty-Eight Constellations Tortoise,' the sixth the 'Sun and Moon Tortoise,' the seventh the 'Nine Provinces Tortoise,' the eighth the 'Jade Tortoise' — eight named tortoises in all.
translations.idiomatic: The first is called the Northern Dipper Tortoise; the second, the Southern Star Tortoise; the third, the Five Planets Tortoise; the fourth, the Eight Winds Tortoise; the fifth, the Twenty-Eight Mansions Tortoise; the sixth, the Sun and Moon Tortoise; the seventh, the Nine Provinces Tortoise; and the eighth, the Jade Tortoise — eight named tortoises in all.

sentences.zh: 龜圖各有文在腹下，文云云者，此某之龜也。

translations.literal: The tortoise diagrams each have markings on the underside of the belly; the markings that read such-and-such indicate that this is such-and-such a tortoise.
translations.idiomatic: Each type of tortoise has distinctive markings on the underside of its shell. The particular pattern indicates to which class the tortoise belongs.

translations.literal: Here I briefly record their general meaning but do not copy their diagrams.
translations.idiomatic: Here I record only the general purport and do not reproduce the diagrams.

sentences.zh: 取此龜不必滿尺二寸，民人得長七八寸，可寶矣。

translations.literal: In obtaining these tortoises, one need not have a full one chi two cun; if ordinary people obtain one seven or eight cun long, it can be treasured.
translations.idiomatic: One need not find a tortoise a full one chi and two cun in length. For ordinary folk, a tortoise of seven or eight cun is a treasure well worth keeping.

sentences.zh: 今夫珠玉寶器，雖有所深藏，必見其光，必出其神明，其此之謂乎！

translations.literal: Now, as for pearls, jade, and precious vessels, even when deeply hidden away, their radiance must be seen and their divine brilliance must emerge — is this not what is meant!
translations.idiomatic: Even pearls, jade, and precious vessels, though buried deep, will inevitably reveal their radiance and manifest their spiritual luminance. Is this not what the saying refers to!

sentences.zh: 笔玉處於山而木潤，淵生珠而岸不枯者，潤澤之所加也。

translations.literal: When jade resides in the mountain the trees are lush, and when an abyss produces pearls the banks do not wither — this is the effect of their moistening influence.
translations.idiomatic: Where jade dwells within the mountain, the trees grow lush; where an abyss produces pearls, the riverbanks never wither. Such is the nourishing influence these treasures exert.

sentences.zh: 明月之珠出於江海，藏於蚌中，蚗龍伏之。

translations.literal: The bright moon pearl comes from the rivers and seas, hidden within a clam, with a hornless dragon coiled over it.
translations.idiomatic: The luminous pearl is born in the rivers and seas, concealed within a clam, with a hornless dragon coiled above to guard it.

sentences.zh: 王者得之，長有天下，四夷賓服。

translations.literal: A king who obtains it will long possess the realm, and the four barbarian peoples will submit as guests.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler who obtains it will hold the empire for ages to come, and the barbarian peoples of the four quarters will pledge their submission.

sentences.zh: 能得百莖蓍，并得其下龜以卜者，百言百當，足以決吉凶。

translations.literal: One who can obtain milfoil of a hundred stalks, and also obtain the tortoise beneath it to use for divination, will be correct in a hundred out of a hundred predictions, sufficient to determine fortune and misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever obtains milfoil of a hundred stalks together with the tortoise that guards it below, and uses them for divination, will find every prediction fulfilled — more than sufficient to determine fortune and misfortune.

sentences.zh: 神龜出於江水中，廬江郡常歲時生龜長尺二寸者二十枚輸太卜官，太卜官因以吉日剔取其腹下甲。

translations.literal: Divine tortoises come from the river waters. The Commandery of Lujiang regularly delivers twenty tortoises one chi two cun long each year to the Grand Diviner's office, and the Grand Diviner's office then selects an auspicious day to pry off the shell from their underbellies.
translations.idiomatic: Divine tortoises are found in the rivers. Each year, the Commandery of Lujiang delivers twenty tortoises, each one chi and two cun in length, to the office of the Grand Diviner. The Grand Diviner then chooses an auspicious day to remove the plastron from beneath each tortoise.

translations.literal: A tortoise must be a thousand years old to reach a full one chi two cun.
translations.idiomatic: A tortoise must live a thousand years before it reaches a full one chi and two cun in length.

sentences.zh: 王者發軍行將，必鉆龜廟堂之上，以決吉凶。

translations.literal: When a king dispatches armies and sends generals on campaigns, he must drill a tortoise shell in the ancestral temple to determine fortune and misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a king dispatches armies and sends generals forth on campaign, he must first bore into a tortoise shell in the ancestral temple to determine whether fortune or misfortune awaits.

sentences.zh: 今高廟中有龜室，藏內以為神寶。

translations.literal: Now in the Temple of Gaozu there is a tortoise chamber, where they are stored inside as divine treasures.
translations.idiomatic: To this day, within the Temple of Gaozu there is a tortoise chamber where the shells are kept as sacred treasures.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「取前足臑骨穿佩之，取龜置室西北隅懸之，以入深山大林中，不惑。」

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'Take the foreleg shank bone, bore a hole in it and wear it as a pendant; take the tortoise and hang it in the northwest corner of the room — then one may enter deep mountains and great forests without becoming lost.'
translations.idiomatic: The tradition states: 'Take the foreleg shank bone, bore a hole through it, and wear it as a pendant. Hang the tortoise in the northwest corner of the room. Then one may enter the deepest mountains and densest forests without losing the way.'

sentences.zh: 臣為郎時，見萬畢石朱方，傳曰：「有神龜在江南嘉林中。

translations.literal: When your servant was a Gentleman, I saw the Wan Bi Shi Zhu Fang, and the tradition says: 'There is a divine tortoise in the auspicious forest south of the Yangtze.
translations.idiomatic: When I was serving as a Gentleman, I came across the Wanbi Recipes, which state: 'There is a divine tortoise dwelling in the auspicious forest south of the Yangtze.

sentences.zh: 嘉林者，獸無虎狼，鳥無鴟梟，草無毒螫，野火不及，斧斤不至，是為嘉林。

translations.literal: An auspicious forest is one where among the animals there are no tigers or wolves, among the birds no kites or owls, among the grasses no poisonous or stinging plants, where wildfire does not reach, and axes do not arrive — this is called an auspicious forest.
translations.idiomatic: An auspicious forest is one where no tigers or wolves roam, no kites or owls perch, no poisonous or stinging plants grow, where wildfire cannot reach and no woodsman's axe has ever fallen. Such is what is meant by an auspicious forest.

sentences.zh: 龜在其中，常巢於芳蓮之上。

translations.literal: The tortoise dwells within it, always nesting upon fragrant lotus.
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise dwells within, always nesting atop the fragrant lotus.

sentences.zh: 左脅書文曰：『甲子重光，得我者匹夫為人君，有土正，諸侯得我為帝王。』

translations.literal: On the left flank there is written text that says: 'When the jiazi cycle shines again, whoever obtains me — a commoner will become a lord of men and possess the rectification of territory; a feudal lord who obtains me will become emperor or king.'
translations.idiomatic: On its left flank is inscribed: 'When the jiazi cycle comes round again in doubled radiance, the commoner who obtains me shall become a lord of men with dominion over the land; the feudal lord who obtains me shall become emperor.'

sentences.zh: 求之於白蛇蟠杅林中者，齋戒以待，譺然，狀如有人來告之，因以醮酒佗發，求之三宿而得。」

translations.literal: To seek it in the forest where the white snake coils around a basin, one fasts and waits purified; suddenly, as if someone comes to announce it, one then pours a libation of wine and sets forth to find it — after three nights one obtains it.'
translations.idiomatic: To find it in the forest where the white serpent coils, one must fast and purify oneself and wait. Suddenly, as though someone has come to deliver a message, one pours a libation of wine and sets out. After three nights, the tortoise will be found.'

translations.literal: Viewed from this perspective, is it not magnificent!
translations.idiomatic: Seen in this light, is the tortoise not truly magnificent!

translations.literal: Can the tortoise not be revered?
translations.idiomatic: Can one fail to revere the tortoise?

sentences.zh: 南方老人用龜支床足，行二十餘歲，老人死，移床，龜尚生不死。

translations.literal: An old man of the south used a tortoise to prop up a bed leg for more than twenty years. When the old man died and the bed was moved, the tortoise was still alive and had not died.
translations.idiomatic: An old man in the south once propped up a bed leg with a tortoise, and the arrangement lasted more than twenty years. When the old man died and the bed was moved, the tortoise was still alive.

translations.literal: Tortoises can circulate vital energy and practice guided breathing.
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise possesses the ability to circulate vital energy and practice guided breathing exercises.

sentences.zh: 問者曰：「龜至神若此，然太卜官得生龜，何為輒殺取其甲乎？」

translations.literal: A questioner asks: 'If the tortoise is as divine as this, then when the Grand Diviner's office obtains a living tortoise, why do they always kill it to take its shell?'
translations.idiomatic: One might ask: 'If the tortoise is truly this divine, then why does the Grand Diviner's office, upon obtaining a living tortoise, invariably kill it to take its shell?'

sentences.zh: 近世江上人有得名龜，畜置之，家因大富。

translations.literal: In recent times, a man living by the river obtained a famous tortoise, kept it, and his family consequently grew very wealthy.
translations.idiomatic: In recent times, a man living by the river obtained a famous tortoise and kept it as a pet. His household grew enormously wealthy as a result.

translations.literal: He discussed with others and wished to send it away.
translations.idiomatic: He consulted with others and considered releasing the tortoise.

sentences.zh: 人教殺之勿遣，遣之破人家。

translations.literal: People taught him to kill it and not release it, saying that releasing it would ruin his family.
translations.idiomatic: Others advised him to kill it rather than release it, warning that setting it free would bring ruin upon his household.

sentences.zh: 龜見夢曰：「送我水中，無殺吾也。」

translations.literal: The tortoise appeared in a dream saying: 'Return me to the water; do not kill me.'
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise appeared to him in a dream and said: 'Return me to the water. Do not kill me.'

translations.literal: His family ultimately killed it.
translations.idiomatic: In the end, the family killed the tortoise.

translations.literal: After killing it, the man himself died, and the family suffered misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: After the tortoise was killed, the man himself died, and misfortune befell the household.

translations.literal: Common people and kings follow different principles.
translations.idiomatic: The way of the common people differs from that of kings.

sentences.zh: 人民得名龜，其狀類不宜殺也。

translations.literal: When common people obtain a famous tortoise, its nature and kind suggest it should not be killed.
translations.idiomatic: When ordinary folk obtain a famous tortoise, all signs suggest it ought not to be killed.

sentences.zh: 以往古故事言之，古明王聖主皆殺而用之。

translations.literal: Speaking from the precedents of the ancient past, the enlightened kings and sage rulers of old all killed them and used them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet if we look to the precedents of antiquity, the enlightened kings and sage rulers of old invariably killed them and put them to use.

sentences.zh: 宋元王時得龜，亦殺而用之。

translations.literal: When King Yuan of Song obtained a tortoise, he also killed it and used it.
translations.idiomatic: When King Yuan of Song obtained a tortoise, he too killed it and put it to use.

sentences.zh: 謹連其事於左方，令好事者觀擇其中焉。

translations.literal: I respectfully connect the account of those events below, so that those who are interested may examine and select from them.
translations.idiomatic: I set down the account of those events in what follows, so that interested readers may examine them for themselves.

sentences.zh: 宋元王二年，江使神龜使於河，至於泉陽，漁者豫且舉網得而囚之。

translations.literal: In the second year of King Yuan of Song, the Yangtze sent a divine tortoise on a mission to the Yellow River. When it reached Quanyang, the fisherman Yu Qie cast his net and caught and imprisoned it.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year of King Yuan of Song, the Yangtze River dispatched a divine tortoise as an envoy to the Yellow River. When it reached Quanyang, a fisherman named Yu Qie cast his net, caught it, and held it captive.

translations.literal: He placed it in a cage.
translations.idiomatic: He placed the tortoise in a basket cage.

sentences.zh: 夜半，龜來見夢於宋元王曰：「我為江使於河，而幕網當吾路。

translations.literal: At midnight, the tortoise came and appeared in a dream to King Yuan of Song, saying: 'I was sent by the Yangtze as an envoy to the Yellow River, but a curtain-net blocked my path.
translations.idiomatic: At midnight, the tortoise appeared in a dream to King Yuan of Song and said: 'I was dispatched by the Yangtze as an envoy to the Yellow River, but a fishing net blocked my path.

sentences.zh: 泉陽豫且得我，我不能去。

translations.literal: Yu Qie of Quanyang caught me, and I cannot escape.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie of Quanyang caught me, and I cannot free myself.

translations.literal: I am trapped in distress, with no one I can tell.
translations.idiomatic: I am trapped in peril, with no one to whom I can appeal.

translations.literal: Your Majesty possesses virtue and righteousness, therefore I have come to inform you.'
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty is a man of virtue and righteousness, and so I have come to plead my case before you.'

translations.literal: King Yuan awoke with a start.
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan awoke with a start, deeply shaken.

sentences.zh: 乃召博士衛平而問之曰：「今寡人夢見一丈夫，延頸而長頭，衣玄繡之衣而乘輜車，來見夢於寡人曰：『我為江使於河，而幕網當吾路。

translations.literal: He then summoned the Erudite Wei Ping and questioned him, saying: 'Just now I dreamed of a man with an outstretched neck and a long head, wearing black embroidered robes and riding in a covered cart. He appeared in my dream and said: "I was sent by the Yangtze as an envoy to the Yellow River, but a curtain-net blocked my path.
translations.idiomatic: He summoned the Erudite Wei Ping and said to him: 'Just now I dreamed of a man with an outstretched neck and elongated head, clad in black embroidered robes and riding in a covered carriage. He appeared in my dream and said: "I was dispatched by the Yangtze as an envoy to the Yellow River, but a fishing net blocked my path.

sentences.zh: 泉陽豫且得我，我不能去。

translations.literal: Yu Qie of Quanyang caught me, and I cannot escape.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie of Quanyang caught me, and I cannot free myself.

translations.literal: I am trapped in distress, with no one I can tell.
translations.idiomatic: I am trapped in peril, with no one to whom I can appeal.

translations.literal: Your Majesty possesses virtue and righteousness, therefore I have come to inform you."
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty is a man of virtue and righteousness, and so I have come to plead my case before you."'

translations.literal: What kind of creature is this?'
translations.idiomatic: What manner of creature could this be?'

sentences.zh: 衛平乃援式而起，仰天而視月之光，觀斗所指，定日處鄉。

translations.literal: Wei Ping then took up his divination board and rose, looked up at the sky to observe the moonlight, noted where the Dipper pointed, and determined the sun's position and direction.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ping took up his divination board and rose to his feet. He looked up to observe the moonlight, noted the direction of the Northern Dipper, and fixed the sun's position and bearing.

translations.literal: The compass and square served as aids, supplemented by the steelyard and balance.
translations.idiomatic: He used the compass and square as his guides, supplemented by the balance and weights.

translations.literal: The four corners were fixed, and the eight trigrams faced one another.
translations.idiomatic: Once the four cardinal points were established and the eight trigrams aligned to face one another,

translations.literal: Observing its fortune and misfortune, the shelled creature appeared first.
translations.idiomatic: he examined the signs of fortune and misfortune, and the shelled creature manifested itself before all others.

sentences.zh: 乃對元王曰：「今昔壬子，宿在牽牛。

translations.literal: He then replied to King Yuan: 'Last night was the renzi day, and the lodge was in the Ox constellation.
translations.idiomatic: He then addressed King Yuan: 'Last night was a renzi day, and the lunar lodge was in the Ox constellation.

translations.literal: The river waters held a great assembly, and the ghosts and spirits conferred with one another.
translations.idiomatic: The river waters gathered for a great assembly, and the spirits convened to deliberate.

sentences.zh: 漢正南北，江河固期，南風新至，江使先來。

translations.literal: The Milky Way is aligned due north-south, the Yangtze and Yellow rivers have a fixed appointment, the south wind has just arrived, and the envoy from the Yangtze came first.
translations.idiomatic: The Milky Way stands aligned from north to south, the Yangtze and the Yellow River had a fixed appointment, the south wind has just arrived, and the envoy from the Yangtze came ahead of schedule.

translations.literal: White clouds blocked the Milky Way, and all things were held back.
translations.idiomatic: White clouds choked the Milky Way, and all things were brought to a halt.

translations.literal: The handle of the Dipper points toward the sun, and the envoy should be imprisoned.
translations.idiomatic: The handle of the Dipper points toward the sun — the envoy was fated to be captured.

sentences.zh: 玄服而乘輜車，其名為龜。

translations.literal: Wearing black robes and riding in a covered cart — its name is tortoise.
translations.idiomatic: Clad in black robes and riding in a covered carriage — the creature you saw is a tortoise.

translations.literal: Your Majesty should urgently send someone to inquire and seek it.'
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty should dispatch someone at once to make inquiries and recover it.'

translations.literal: The king said: 'Good.'
translations.idiomatic: The king said: 'Very well.'

sentences.zh: 於是王乃使人馳而往問泉陽令曰：「漁者幾何家？

translations.literal: Thereupon the king sent a man galloping to go and question the magistrate of Quanyang, saying: 'How many fishing households are there?
translations.idiomatic: The king immediately dispatched a messenger at a gallop to the magistrate of Quanyang with this inquiry: 'How many fishing households are there?

translations.literal: Which one is named Yu Qie?
translations.idiomatic: Which of them is called Yu Qie?

sentences.zh: 豫且得龜，見夢於王，王故使我求之。」

translations.literal: Yu Qie caught a tortoise, which appeared in a dream to the king; the king therefore sends me to seek it.'
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie caught a tortoise, and it appeared to the king in a dream. The king has therefore sent me to recover it.'

sentences.zh: 泉陽令乃使吏案籍視圖，水上漁者五十五家，上流之廬，名為豫且。

translations.literal: The magistrate of Quanyang then had his clerks examine the registers and maps; there were fifty-five fishing households on the water, and at the hut upstream, the name was Yu Qie.
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate of Quanyang ordered his clerks to examine the registers and maps. There were fifty-five fishing households on the water, and the dwelling upstream belonged to a man named Yu Qie.

translations.literal: The magistrate of Quanyang said: 'Understood.'
translations.idiomatic: The magistrate of Quanyang replied: 'Understood.'

sentences.zh: 乃與使者馳而問豫且曰：「今昔汝漁何得？」

translations.literal: He then galloped with the messenger to question Yu Qie, saying: 'What did you catch in your fishing last night?'
translations.idiomatic: He rode out with the messenger to find Yu Qie and asked him: 'What did you catch in your nets last night?'

sentences.zh: 豫且曰：「夜半時舉網得龜。」

translations.literal: Yu Qie said: 'At midnight I raised my net and caught a tortoise.'
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie replied: 'At midnight I hauled up my net and found a tortoise.'

translations.literal: The messenger said: 'Where is the tortoise now?'
translations.idiomatic: The messenger asked: 'Where is the tortoise now?'

translations.literal: He said: 'It is in the cage.'
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie replied: 'It is in the basket cage.'

sentences.zh: 使者曰：「王知子得龜，故使我求之。」

translations.literal: The messenger said: 'The king knows you caught a tortoise, and therefore sent me to retrieve it.'
translations.idiomatic: The messenger said: 'The king knows that you caught a tortoise, and has sent me to retrieve it.'

translations.literal: Yu Qie said: 'Understood.'
translations.idiomatic: Yu Qie replied: 'As you wish.'

sentences.zh: 即系龜而出之籠中，獻使者。

translations.literal: He then tied up the tortoise and took it out of the cage, presenting it to the messenger.
translations.idiomatic: He tied up the tortoise, brought it out of the cage, and presented it to the messenger.

sentences.zh: 使者載行，出於泉陽之門。

translations.literal: The messenger loaded it on his carriage and departed, passing through the gate of Quanyang.
translations.idiomatic: The messenger loaded the tortoise onto his carriage and departed through the gate of Quanyang.

translations.literal: At midday nothing could be seen; wind and rain brought darkness and gloom.
translations.idiomatic: Though it was broad daylight, nothing could be seen. Wind and rain plunged everything into darkness.

translations.literal: Clouds covered above, in five colors of blue-green and yellow;
translations.idiomatic: Clouds canopied overhead, shimmering in five colors of azure and gold;

translations.literal: Thunder and rain arose together, and the wind carried them along.
translations.idiomatic: thunder and rain erupted simultaneously, and the wind swept them onward.

translations.literal: They entered through the main gate and appeared in the eastern chamber.
translations.idiomatic: They passed through the main gate and presented themselves in the eastern wing of the palace.

translations.literal: Its body was like flowing water, moist, lustrous, and radiant.
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise's body gleamed like flowing water, moist and luminous.

sentences.zh: 望見元王，延頸而前，三步而止，縮頸而卻，復其故處。

translations.literal: When it saw King Yuan in the distance, it stretched out its neck and advanced, took three steps and stopped, retracted its neck and retreated, returning to its original place.
translations.idiomatic: When it caught sight of King Yuan, the tortoise stretched out its neck and advanced three steps, then stopped, drew its neck back, retreated, and returned to its original spot.

sentences.zh: 元王見而怪之，問衛平曰：「龜見寡人，延頸而前，以何望也？

translations.literal: King Yuan saw this and found it strange. He asked Wei Ping: 'The tortoise saw me and stretched its neck forward — what was it hoping for?
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan found this behavior strange and asked Wei Ping: 'The tortoise stretched out its neck and advanced toward me — what was it hoping for?

translations.literal: It retracted its neck and returned — what does this signify?'
translations.idiomatic: Then it drew back its neck and retreated. What does this signify?'

sentences.zh: 衛平對曰：「龜在患中，而終昔囚，王有德義，使人活之。

translations.literal: Wei Ping replied: 'The tortoise was in distress, imprisoned throughout the previous night. The king possesses virtue and righteousness, and sent someone to rescue it.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ping replied: 'The tortoise was in peril, held captive through the night. Your Majesty, being a man of virtue and righteousness, sent someone to rescue it.

sentences.zh: 今延頸而前，以當謝也，縮頸而卻，欲亟去也。」

translations.literal: Now it stretched its neck forward to express gratitude, and retracted its neck and retreated because it wishes to leave quickly.'
translations.idiomatic: By stretching its neck forward, it was expressing gratitude. By drawing back and retreating, it was signaling its desire to depart at once.'

translations.literal: King Yuan said: 'Excellent!
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan exclaimed: 'How remarkable!

translations.literal: Its divinity reaches to this extent — it must not be detained long;
translations.idiomatic: Its spiritual power is truly this great — we must not keep it any longer;

translations.literal: hasten to prepare a carriage and send the tortoise on its way; do not let it miss its appointed time.'
translations.idiomatic: ready a carriage at once and send the tortoise on its way, lest it miss its appointed time.'

sentences.zh: 衛平對曰：「龜者是天下之寶也，先得此龜者為天子，且十言十當，十戰十勝。

translations.literal: Wei Ping replied: 'This tortoise is a treasure of the realm. Whoever first obtains it shall become Son of Heaven; moreover, ten pronouncements will yield ten correct outcomes, and ten battles will yield ten victories.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ping replied: 'This tortoise is a treasure of all under Heaven. Whoever first obtains such a tortoise shall become the Son of Heaven. With it, ten pronouncements will yield ten correct results, and ten battles will end in ten victories.

translations.literal: Born in the deep abyss, it grew up in the yellow earth.
translations.idiomatic: Born in the depths of the abyss, it was nurtured in the yellow earth.

translations.literal: It knows the Way of Heaven and is versed in high antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: It comprehends the Way of Heaven and is learned in the ways of high antiquity.

translations.literal: It has roamed for three thousand years without leaving its domain.
translations.idiomatic: For three thousand years it has roamed without venturing beyond its domain.

translations.literal: Peaceful, calm, still, and upright, it moves without exerting force.
translations.idiomatic: Tranquil, serene, still, and upright, it moves without the slightest exertion.

translations.literal: Its lifespan covers heaven and earth, and no one knows its limit.
translations.idiomatic: Its lifespan rivals that of heaven and earth, and none can fathom its limits.

translations.literal: It transforms along with all things, changing color with the four seasons.
translations.idiomatic: It transforms in concert with all things, changing its color with the turning of the four seasons.

translations.literal: It dwells in concealment and lies dormant without eating.
translations.idiomatic: It dwells in concealment, lying dormant and taking no food.

translations.literal: In spring it is blue-green, in summer yellow, in autumn white, in winter black.
translations.idiomatic: In spring it turns blue-green, in summer yellow, in autumn white, and in winter black.

translations.literal: It is versed in yin and yang, and discerning in punishment and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: It is versed in the principles of yin and yang, and discerning in the arts of punishment and benevolence.

sentences.zh: 先知利害，察於禍福，以言而當，以戰而勝，王能寶之，諸侯盡服。

translations.literal: It foreknows benefit and harm, discerns fortune and calamity; with its pronouncements one is always right, with its counsel in war one always prevails. If the king can treasure it, all the feudal lords will submit.
translations.idiomatic: It foresees advantage and harm, discerns fortune from calamity. With its counsel, every pronouncement proves correct; with its guidance in war, every battle ends in victory. If Your Majesty treasures it, all the feudal lords will submit.'

translations.literal: Your Majesty should not release it, so as to secure the altars of state.'
translations.idiomatic: Do not release it, Your Majesty, but keep it to safeguard the altars of state.'

sentences.zh: 元王曰：「龜甚神靈，降于上天，陷於深淵。

translations.literal: King Yuan said: 'The tortoise is supremely divine and spiritual; it descended from the heavens above and sank into the deep abyss.
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan replied: 'This tortoise is a supremely divine creature. It descended from the heavens above and plunged into the deepest abyss.

translations.literal: It was in distress and difficulty.
translations.idiomatic: It found itself in peril and distress.

translations.literal: It considered me worthy.
translations.idiomatic: It deemed me a worthy man.

sentences.zh: 德厚而忠信，故來告寡人。

translations.literal: Possessing deep virtue, loyalty, and trust, it therefore came to inform me.
translations.idiomatic: Perceiving my deep virtue, loyalty, and trustworthiness, it came to plead its case before me.

sentences.zh: 寡人若不遣也，是漁者也。

translations.literal: If I do not release it, then I am no different from a fisherman.
translations.idiomatic: If I were not to release it, I would be no better than the fisherman.

sentences.zh: 漁者利其肉，寡人貪其力，下為不仁，上為無德。

translations.literal: The fisherman covets its flesh, and I would covet its power; below, this is inhumanity; above, it is a lack of virtue.
translations.idiomatic: The fisherman covets its flesh, and I would covet its power. For those below, this is inhumanity; for those above, it is a lack of virtue.

translations.literal: When ruler and minister lack propriety, from where will good fortune come?
translations.idiomatic: When ruler and minister alike abandon propriety, how can good fortune possibly follow?

translations.literal: I cannot bear it — how can I not release it!'
translations.idiomatic: I cannot bear to act otherwise. How could I possibly refuse to release it!'

translations.literal: Wei Ping replied: 'That is not so.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ping responded: 'That is not how things stand.

sentences.zh: 臣聞盛德不報，重寄不歸；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that great virtue need not be repaid, and a weighty entrustment need not be returned;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard it said that supreme virtue does not demand repayment, and a great trust does not require its return;

translations.literal: When Heaven gives and one does not accept, Heaven takes away the treasure.
translations.idiomatic: when Heaven bestows a gift and one refuses to accept it, Heaven withdraws the treasure.

sentences.zh: 今龜周流天下，還復其所，上至蒼天，下薄泥涂。

translations.literal: Now this tortoise has circled through all under Heaven, returning always to its place; above it ascends to the blue sky, below it sinks to the mire.
translations.idiomatic: This tortoise has roamed the entire world and always returns to its place. Above, it ascends to the azure sky; below, it descends into the mire.

sentences.zh: 還遍九州，未嘗愧辱，無所稽留。

translations.literal: It has traversed the Nine Provinces, never suffering shame or humiliation, never being detained.
translations.idiomatic: It has traversed the breadth of the Nine Provinces, never once suffering shame or humiliation, never once being detained.

sentences.zh: 今至泉陽，漁者辱而囚之。

translations.literal: Now, arriving at Quanyang, a fisherman humiliated and imprisoned it.
translations.idiomatic: Yet upon arriving at Quanyang, a fisherman humiliated it and held it captive.

sentences.zh: 王雖遣之，江河必怒，務求報仇。

translations.literal: Even if the king releases it, the Yangtze and Yellow rivers will surely be enraged, and will seek revenge.
translations.idiomatic: Even if Your Majesty releases it, the Yangtze and the Yellow River will surely be enraged and bent on retribution.

translations.literal: They will consider themselves violated, and they will conspire with the spirits.
translations.idiomatic: Deeming themselves affronted, they will conspire with the spirits.

translations.literal: Excessive rains will not cease, and the waters will be uncontrollable.
translations.idiomatic: Torrential rains will fall without end, and the floodwaters will be beyond control.

sentences.zh: 若為枯旱，風而揚埃，蝗蟲暴生，百姓失時。

translations.literal: Or there will be drought, winds raising dust, locusts suddenly swarming, and the common people losing their harvest seasons.
translations.idiomatic: Or else they will bring drought, with winds whipping up the dust, locusts swarming in sudden plagues, and the common people losing their harvests.

translations.literal: If the king practices benevolence and righteousness in this way, their punishment will surely come.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty persists in this course of benevolence and righteousness, their punishment will surely follow.

translations.literal: There is no other reason for this — the curse lies in the tortoise.
translations.idiomatic: There is no other cause — the curse originates with the tortoise.

translations.literal: Though you may later regret it, how will it be in time!
translations.idiomatic: Even should you regret it afterward, it will be far too late!

translations.literal: Do not release it, Your Majesty.'
translations.idiomatic: I implore Your Majesty: do not release it.'

sentences.zh: 元王慨然而嘆曰：「夫逆人之使，絕人之謀，是不暴乎？

translations.literal: King Yuan sighed with emotion and said: 'To obstruct another's envoy and cut off another's plans — is this not tyranny?
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan heaved a deep sigh and said: 'To obstruct another's envoy and thwart another's designs — is this not tyranny?

sentences.zh: 取人之有，以自為寶，是不彊乎？

translations.literal: To seize what belongs to another and make it one's own treasure — is this not oppression?
translations.idiomatic: To seize another's possession and claim it as one's own treasure — is this not brute force?

sentences.zh: 寡人聞之，暴得者必暴亡，彊取者必后無功。

translations.literal: I have heard that what is gained through tyranny will surely be lost through tyranny, and what is taken by force will ultimately bring no merit.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard it said that what is gained through tyranny will be lost through tyranny, and what is seized by force will in the end bring no lasting gain.

translations.literal: Jie and Zhou were tyrannical and oppressive — they themselves died and their states perished.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou ruled through tyranny and violence — they perished, and their states fell with them.

sentences.zh: 今我聽子，是無仁義之名而有暴彊之道。

translations.literal: If I now listen to you, I will lose the name of benevolence and righteousness and adopt the way of tyranny and force.
translations.idiomatic: If I heed your counsel, I will forfeit the name of benevolence and righteousness and follow the path of tyranny and force.

translations.literal: The Yangtze and Yellow rivers would be like Tang and Wu, and I would be like Jie and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The Yangtze and the Yellow River would stand as Tang and Wu, and I would be cast as Jie and Zhou.

translations.literal: I see no advantage, and I fear incurring blame.
translations.idiomatic: I see no advantage in it, and I fear the blame it would bring upon me.

sentences.zh: 寡人狐疑，安事此寶，趣駕送龜，勿令久留。」

translations.literal: I am filled with doubt — what use is this treasure? Hasten to prepare a carriage and send the tortoise away; do not let it be detained long.'
translations.idiomatic: My mind is troubled with doubt. What use is such a treasure to me? Ready a carriage at once and send the tortoise on its way. It must not be detained any longer.'

sentences.zh: 衛平對曰：「不然，王其無患。

translations.literal: Wei Ping replied: 'That is not so. May the king have no worries.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Ping replied: 'That is not how things stand. Let Your Majesty put aside these worries.

translations.literal: Between heaven and earth, stones are piled to form mountains.
translations.idiomatic: Between heaven and earth, stones are heaped up to form mountains.

translations.literal: Though high, they do not collapse, and the earth is made stable.
translations.idiomatic: Towering yet never collapsing, they render the earth stable.

sentences.zh: 故云物或危而顧安，或輕而不可遷；

translations.literal: Thus it is said that some things seem precarious yet prove secure, while others seem light yet cannot be moved;
translations.idiomatic: Thus it is said that some things appear precarious yet prove perfectly secure, and some things seem light yet cannot be moved;

sentences.zh: 人或忠信而不如誕謾，或醜惡而宜大官，或美好佳麗而為眾人患。

translations.literal: some people are loyal and trustworthy yet fare worse than liars and cheats; some are ugly and vile yet suited for high office; some are beautiful and lovely yet become a bane to all.
translations.idiomatic: some people are loyal and trustworthy yet fare worse than deceivers; some are ugly and base yet prove fit for high office; and some are beautiful and enchanting yet become a curse upon all around them.

translations.literal: Unless one is a divine sage, no one can fully express it.
translations.idiomatic: Unless one is a divine sage, no one can fully articulate these truths.

translations.literal: Spring, autumn, winter, summer — now hot, now cold.
translations.idiomatic: Through spring, autumn, winter, and summer — now scorching, now freezing.

translations.literal: When cold and heat are out of harmony, noxious vapors intrude upon one another.
translations.idiomatic: When cold and heat fall out of harmony, noxious vapors encroach upon one another.

translations.literal: Within the same year the seasons differ — it is their appointed time that makes it so.
translations.idiomatic: Within a single year the seasons differ — the appointed cycle of time ordains it thus.

sentences.zh: 故令春生夏長，秋收冬藏。

translations.literal: Therefore spring brings birth, summer growth, autumn harvest, winter storage.
translations.idiomatic: And so spring gives birth, summer nurtures growth, autumn gathers the harvest, and winter stores it away.

translations.literal: Some practice benevolence and righteousness, some practice tyranny and force.
translations.idiomatic: Some follow the way of benevolence and righteousness; others follow the way of tyranny and force.

translations.literal: Tyranny and force have their direction, benevolence and righteousness have their season.
translations.idiomatic: Tyranny and force have their place, and benevolence and righteousness have their appointed time.

translations.literal: All the myriad things are thus — they cannot be fully governed.
translations.idiomatic: All things in creation follow this pattern — they are beyond the power of any one ruler to fully control.

translations.literal: If the great king will listen to your servant, your servant asks to speak fully on the matter.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty will hear me out, allow me to speak in full.'

translations.literal: Heaven produces the five colors to distinguish white from black.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven brings forth the five colors so that white may be distinguished from black.

translations.literal: Earth produces the five grains so that good and evil may be known.
translations.idiomatic: Earth produces the five grains so that good may be known from evil.

sentences.zh: 人民莫知辨也，與禽獸相若。

translations.literal: The people did not know how to distinguish, and they were no different from birds and beasts.
translations.idiomatic: The people knew no way to make such distinctions and were scarcely different from birds and beasts.

translations.literal: They dwelt in valleys and lived in caves, not knowing how to cultivate fields.
translations.idiomatic: They dwelt in valleys and sheltered in caves, ignorant of agriculture.

translations.literal: The realm was plagued by calamity and chaos, with yin and yang thrown into confusion.
translations.idiomatic: Calamity and chaos reigned throughout the world, and yin and yang were thrown into disorder.

translations.literal: In haste and urgency, all mingled together without discrimination.
translations.idiomatic: All was haste and confusion, with everything mingling indiscriminately.

translations.literal: Monstrous portents appeared frequently, and traditions grew thin and weak.
translations.idiomatic: Monstrous portents appeared with alarming frequency, and the inherited traditions grew thin and feeble.

translations.literal: The sages separated the living creatures, ensuring they would not prey upon one another.
translations.idiomatic: The sages sorted the living creatures into their proper kinds, ensuring they would not prey upon one another.

translations.literal: Beasts having male and female were placed in the mountains and plains;
translations.idiomatic: Beasts, with their males and females, were assigned to the mountains and plains;

translations.literal: Birds having male and female were distributed in forests and marshes;
translations.idiomatic: birds, with their males and females, were dispersed through the forests and marshlands;

translations.literal: Shelled creatures were placed in streams and valleys.
translations.idiomatic: and shelled creatures were consigned to the streams and valleys.

sentences.zh: 故牧人民，為之城郭，內經閭術，外為阡陌。

translations.literal: Therefore to shepherd the people, they built city walls and ramparts, within which they laid out neighborhoods and roads, and without which they established field paths and boundaries.
translations.idiomatic: To shepherd the people, the sages built walled cities, laying out neighborhoods and streets within, and establishing field paths and boundaries without.

sentences.zh: 夫妻男女，賦之田宅，列其室屋。

translations.literal: Husbands and wives, men and women, were allotted fields and residences, and their households were arranged in rows.
translations.idiomatic: Husbands and wives, men and women, were allotted fields and residences, their dwellings arranged in orderly rows.

translations.literal: They created registers and records, distinguishing names and clans.
translations.idiomatic: Registers and records were created, distinguishing each family by name and lineage.

translations.literal: They established officials and appointed clerks, encouraging them with ranks and stipends.
translations.idiomatic: Officials were established and clerks appointed, encouraged by ranks and stipends.

translations.literal: They clothed them with mulberry silk and hemp, and nourished them with the five grains.
translations.idiomatic: They clothed the people in silk and hemp and nourished them with the five grains.

translations.literal: They plowed and harrowed, hoed and weeded.
translations.idiomatic: They plowed the soil, broke the clods, hoed the rows, and cleared the weeds.

sentences.zh: 口得所嗜，目得所美，身受其利。

translations.literal: The mouth obtained what it craved, the eye obtained what it found beautiful, and the body received its benefit.
translations.idiomatic: The mouth could savor what it craved, the eye could behold what it found beautiful, and the body reaped the benefits.

translations.literal: Viewed from this perspective, none of this can be achieved without force.
translations.idiomatic: Seen in this light, nothing can be accomplished without the exercise of force.

sentences.zh: 故曰田者不彊，囷倉不盈；

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: if the farmer is not forceful, the granaries will not be full;
translations.idiomatic: Thus it is said: if the farmer does not exert himself, the granaries will stand empty;

translations.literal: if the merchant is not forceful, he will not gain his profit;
translations.idiomatic: if the merchant does not exert himself, he will gain no profit;

translations.literal: if women are not forceful, their cloth and silk will not be fine;
translations.idiomatic: if women do not exert themselves, their cloth and silk will not be fine;

translations.literal: if officials do not exert themselves, their authority will not be established;
translations.idiomatic: if officials do not exert themselves, their authority will not hold;

translations.literal: if a great general is not forceful, his soldiers will not obey his commands;
translations.idiomatic: if a general does not exert himself, his soldiers will not follow orders;

translations.literal: if a lord or king is not forceful, he will die without a name.
translations.idiomatic: and if a lord or king does not exert himself, he will pass from the world without leaving a name.

sentences.zh: 故云彊者，事之始也，分之理也，物之紀也。

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: forceful exertion is the beginning of enterprise, the principle of proper distinction, and the binding thread of all things.
translations.idiomatic: Therefore it is said: exertion is the wellspring of all enterprise, the principle underlying all distinction, and the binding thread that holds all things together.

translations.literal: Whatever is sought through forceful effort, there is nothing that cannot be obtained.
translations.idiomatic: Whatever is pursued through effort, there is nothing that cannot be attained.

sentences.zh: 王以為不然，王獨不聞玉櫝隻雉，出於昆山；

translations.literal: If the king thinks otherwise, has the king alone not heard that jade caskets and lone pheasants come from Mount Kunlun;
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty disagrees, consider this: have you not heard that jade caskets and rare pheasant plumes come from Mount Kunlun;

translations.literal: luminous pearls come from the four seas;
translations.idiomatic: that luminous pearls come from the four seas;

translations.literal: they chisel stone and pry open clams, then pass them on for sale in the market;
translations.idiomatic: that stone is chiseled and clams pried open, and the treasures passed on for sale in the markets;

translations.literal: When sages obtain these, they make them their greatest treasures.
translations.idiomatic: When sages obtain such things, they make them the greatest of treasures.

translations.literal: Wherever the greatest treasures reside, there is the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever the greatest treasures reside, there stands the Son of Heaven.

sentences.zh: 今王自以為暴，不如拌蚌於海也；

translations.literal: If the king considers himself tyrannical, this is no worse than prying open clams in the sea;
translations.idiomatic: If Your Majesty considers this an act of tyranny, it is no worse than prying open clams in the sea;

sentences.zh: 自以為彊，不過鐫石於昆山也。

translations.literal: if he considers himself forceful, this is no more than chiseling stone from Mount Kunlun.
translations.idiomatic: and if Your Majesty considers it an act of force, it is no more than quarrying stone from Mount Kunlun.

translations.literal: Those who take incur no blame, and those who treasure incur no calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Those who take such treasures incur no blame, and those who keep them suffer no calamity.

sentences.zh: 今龜使來抵網，而遭漁者得之，見夢自言，是國之寶也，王何憂焉。」

translations.literal: Now the tortoise-envoy came and ran into a net, and happened to be caught by a fisherman; it appeared in a dream and spoke of itself — this is a treasure of the state. What does the king have to worry about?'
translations.idiomatic: Now the tortoise-envoy blundered into a net and was caught by a fisherman. It appeared in a dream and pleaded its own case — this is a treasure of the state. What has Your Majesty to fear?'

translations.literal: King Yuan said: 'That is not so.
translations.idiomatic: King Yuan replied: 'That is not how I see it.

sentences.zh: 寡人聞之，諫者福也，諛者賊也。

translations.literal: I have heard that those who remonstrate bring fortune, while those who flatter bring ruin.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard it said that a man who remonstrates brings fortune, while one who flatters brings ruin.

translations.literal: When a ruler listens to flattery, that is foolishness and delusion.
translations.idiomatic: For a ruler to heed flattery is the mark of a fool.

sentences.zh: 雖然，禍不妄至，福不徒來。

translations.literal: Nevertheless, misfortune does not come without cause, and fortune does not arrive without reason.
translations.idiomatic: Be that as it may, misfortune never comes without cause, nor does fortune arrive without reason.

translations.literal: Heaven and earth join their vital energies to produce the hundred kinds of wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven and earth merge their vital energies to bring forth every manner of wealth.

sentences.zh: 陰陽有分，不離四時，十有二月，日至為期。

translations.literal: Yin and yang have their divisions and do not depart from the four seasons; the twelve months take the solstice as their marker.
translations.idiomatic: Yin and yang follow their appointed divisions and do not depart from the four seasons. The twelve months take the solstice as their turning point.

translations.literal: The sage comprehends this thoroughly, and so his person suffers no disaster.
translations.idiomatic: The sage who thoroughly comprehends these principles keeps himself free from disaster.

translations.literal: When an enlightened king employs this, no one dares to deceive him.
translations.idiomatic: When an enlightened king applies these principles, no one dares deceive him.

sentences.zh: 故云福之至也，人自生之；

translations.literal: Therefore it is said: when fortune arrives, it is man himself who brings it about;
translations.idiomatic: Thus it is said: fortune, when it comes, is something a man creates for himself;

translations.literal: when misfortune arrives, it is man himself who creates it.
translations.idiomatic: and misfortune, when it comes, is something a man brings upon himself.

translations.literal: Misfortune and fortune are companions; punishment and virtue are a pair.
translations.idiomatic: Misfortune and fortune are two sides of the same coin, as are punishment and virtue.

translations.literal: The sage examines them to discern fortune and misfortune.
translations.idiomatic: The sage scrutinizes these to distinguish fortune from misfortune.

sentences.zh: 桀紂之時，與天爭功，擁遏鬼神，使不得通。

translations.literal: In the times of Jie and Zhou, they contended with Heaven for merit, obstructed and blocked the ghosts and spirits, preventing them from communicating.
translations.idiomatic: In the time of Jie and Zhou, they vied with Heaven itself for credit, obstructed the spirits, and barred them from communion with the world.

sentences.zh: 是固已無道矣，諛臣有眾。

translations.literal: This was already the height of lawlessness, yet flattering ministers were numerous.
translations.idiomatic: This was already the height of lawlessness, and yet flattering ministers abounded.

translations.literal: Jie had a flattering minister named Zhao Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Jie had a flattering minister by the name of Zhao Liang.

translations.literal: He taught lawless conduct and encouraged greed and rapacity.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Liang instructed him in lawless ways and encouraged his greed and rapacity.

translations.literal: He imprisoned Tang at the Xia Terrace and killed Guan Longfeng.
translations.idiomatic: He imprisoned Tang at the Xia Terrace and put Guan Longfeng to death.

translations.literal: His attendants feared death and secretly offered flattery at his side.
translations.idiomatic: His attendants, fearing death, furtively offered flattery at his side.

translations.literal: The state was in danger like stacked eggs, yet all said there was no harm.
translations.idiomatic: The state teetered as precariously as stacked eggs, yet every voice proclaimed that no harm was at hand.

translations.literal: They proclaimed joy for ten thousand years, or said it was 'unending.'
translations.idiomatic: They proclaimed joy lasting ten thousand years, declaring that the revelry would never end.

translations.literal: They blocked his eyes and ears, joining him in deception and madness.
translations.idiomatic: They blinded his eyes and stopped his ears, abetting him in deceit and madness.

translations.literal: In the end Tang attacked Jie; Jie himself perished and his state was destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: In the end, Tang attacked Jie. Jie perished, and his state was destroyed.

translations.literal: By heeding his flattering ministers, he alone suffered the calamity.
translations.idiomatic: By heeding his flattering ministers, he alone bore the calamity.

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn Annals recorded it, and to this day it has not been forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn Annals set it all down, and to this day the lesson has not been forgotten.

translations.literal: Zhou had a flattering minister named Zuo Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou of Shang had a flattering minister called Zuo Qiang.

translations.literal: He was boastful and had a keen eye for ingenious things, and he taught Zhou to build the Ivory Gallery.
translations.idiomatic: Boastful and sharp-eyed for contrivance, Zuo Qiang taught Zhou to build the Ivory Gallery.

translations.literal: It was to reach to the sky, and there was also a jade bed.
translations.idiomatic: The gallery was to soar to the very heavens, and there was also a bed of jade.

translations.literal: There were vessels of rhinoceros horn and jade, and ivory chopsticks for eating soup.
translations.idiomatic: There were vessels of rhinoceros horn and jade, and ivory chopsticks for the royal meals.

sentences.zh: 聖人剖其心，壯士斬其胻。

translations.literal: He cut open the sage's heart and severed the legs of the brave man.
translations.idiomatic: He cut open the heart of a sage and hacked off the shins of a brave man.

translations.literal: Jizi feared death, so he let down his hair and feigned madness.
translations.idiomatic: Jizi feared for his life, so he let down his hair and feigned madness.

translations.literal: He killed Prince Li of Zhou and imprisoned King Wen, whose name was Chang.
translations.idiomatic: He killed the Zhou prince Li and imprisoned King Wen, whose personal name was Chang.

translations.literal: He threw him into a stone cell, intending to keep him from nightfall to dawn.
translations.idiomatic: He cast him into a stone dungeon, keeping him confined from nightfall to dawn.

translations.literal: Secretly and anxiously they kept him alive, and together they escaped.
translations.idiomatic: His followers secretly and anxiously kept him alive, and together they escaped.

translations.literal: They entered the land of Zhou and obtained the Grand Duke Wang.
translations.idiomatic: They made their way to the land of Zhou, where they found the Grand Duke Wang — Jiang Ziya.

translations.literal: They raised soldiers and gathered troops, and waged war against Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: They raised soldiers and mustered an army to wage war against Zhou of Shang.

translations.literal: King Wen died of illness, and they carried his corpse as they marched.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen fell ill and died, but they carried his body with the army as they marched.

translations.literal: The crown prince Fa took command in his stead and was styled King Wu.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince Fa assumed command and was proclaimed King Wu.

sentences.zh: 戰於牧野，破之華山之陽。

translations.literal: They fought at Muye and defeated him south of Mount Hua.
translations.idiomatic: They clashed at Muye and routed Zhou's forces south of Mount Hua.

sentences.zh: 紂不勝敗而還走，圍之象郎。

translations.literal: Zhou could not prevail in his defeat and fled back, where he was surrounded at the Ivory Gallery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou was routed and fled back to the capital, where he was besieged at the Ivory Gallery.
